risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Individual patients were randomly allocated to one of the three treatment arms using computer generated randomised permuted blocks (with randomly varying block sizes of 9, 12, 15, and 18), after stratification on the basis of whether the patient was non-ambulatory ... and whether the patient fulfilled London ME criteria." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence with permuted blocks and stratification, which is a proper random method and supports low risk of bias for sequence generation.

Quote: "Details of eligible patients were provided by telephone to an independent randomisation service within two working days." Comment: Allocation concealment is explicitly supported because enrollment staff sent eligible participants to an independent randomisation service, which would prevent recruiters from foreseeing the next assignment before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Pragmatic rehabilitation and supportive listening treatments consisted of 10 sessions over an 18 week period" and "patients in the pragmatic rehabilitation and supportive listening arms were free to consult their GP during the course of treatment." Comment: Participants and treating personnel were clearly aware of the assigned intervention because the trial compared distinct talking/rehabilitation therapies with usual care, delivered by nurses who were trained and supervised in the specific treatment. No blinding of participants or therapists is described. Because the main outcomes were self-reported symptoms and functioning (fatigue, physical functioning, sleep, anxiety/depression), knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "Assessments were performed by trained researchers blind to treatment allocation; any unblindings were recorded." Comment: The main outcome assessors were explicitly blinded, and the key outcomes (SF-36 physical functioning, Chalder fatigue, HADS, Jenkins sleep) were collected by these blinded researchers rather than by treating clinicians. Although the outcomes are patient-reported/subjective, the blinding of the researcher conducting assessments reduces the risk of detection bias as described in the report.

Quote: "A total of 257 (87%) of the 296 patients who entered the trial were assessed at 70 weeks." and "We revised our recruitment target to 288 in September 2006 when it became clear that our loss to follow-up was less than anticipated (13% at 70 weeks)." Comment: Attrition was low overall (13% at the primary follow-up), below the 20% threshold, and the paper reports an intention-to-treat analysis plus inverse probability weighting for missing outcomes. This suggests missing data were handled appropriately and are unlikely to have biased the results materially.
